issue: Middle East Weapons of Mass Destruction Free Zone and Israel

entries.position_summary: Russia strongly supports establishing a WMD-free zone in the Middle East, framing it as a top non-proliferation priority. It cites the 2024 BRICS Kazan Declaration endorsing accelerated implementation of relevant resolutions and has circulated a working document to advance the issue.
entries.quote: Unofficial translation STATEMENT by the Delegation of the Russian Federation at the Third Session of the Preparatory Committee for the 11th Review Conference of the Parties to the Treaty on the Non-Proliferation of Nuclear Weapons (Middle East zone free of nuclear weapons and other weapons of mass destruction) (New York, 6 May 2025) Mr. Chair, The Russian Federation has consistently supported the idea of establishing a zone free of nuclear weapons and other weapons of mass destruction (a WMD-free zone) in the Middle East. This issue has traditionally been one of our top foreign policy priorities related to nuclear non-proliferation. This stance was reflected in particular in the Kazan Declaration "Strengthening Multilateralism for Just Global Development and Security" adopted by the BRICS Summit on 23 October 2024 and emphasizing the efforts aiming at accelerating the implementation of the resolutions on the establishment of a zone free of nuclear weapons and other weapons of mass destruction in the Middle East. Ahead of this session of the Preparatory Committee, we circulated a pertinent working document and invite delegations to study it.

entries.position_summary: Russia urges the 2026 NPT Review Conference to recommend that all Middle Eastern states and observers participate in the UN Conference on establishing a WMD-free zone, aiming to accelerate tangible progress toward its early creation.
entries.quote: 15. Taking into account the foregoing, the Russian Federation proposes that the participants in the 2026 Review Conference recommend that all countries of the Middle East without exception, as well as States invited as observers, participate in the United Nations Conference on a WMD-free zone in order to make more active and tangible progress towards the early establishment of such a zone in the region. 25-03566 3/3

entries.position_summary: Russia reaffirms support for establishing a Middle East zone free of nuclear and other WMD as mandated by the 1995 NPT resolution. As a co-sponsor, it acknowledges its responsibility and commits to continue efforts to achieve this goal.
entries.quote: Mr. Chairman, The issue of establishing a zone free from nuclear weapons and other weapons of mass destruction (WMD-free zone) in the Middle East as envisaged by the resolution of the 1995 NPT Review and Extension Conference, has always played special role in the NPT context. Russia, which co-sponsored the resolution, is fully conscious of the responsibility this status implies and intends to continue making all the necessary efforts to reach the objectives of this resolution.

entries.position_summary: The country reaffirms the 1995 NPT Middle East resolution and supports creating a Middle East zone free of nuclear and other WMD. It welcomes the ongoing annual Conference on a Middle East WMD-Free Zone (launched in 2018), participates as an observer, and urges engagement by all regional states and all nuclear-weapon states.
entries.quote: We reaffirm the significance and relevance of the 1995 resolution on the Middle East adopted at the NPT Review and Extension Conference. As one of its co-sponsors, we support the efforts to establish a zone free of nuclear weapons and other weapons of mass destruction (WMD-free zone) in the region. We welcome the process of annual sessions of the Conference on a Middle East WMD-Free zone, which was launched in 2018 and in which Russia takes part as an observer. It is important that all the countries of the region and all nuclear-weapon States engaged in its work.

entries.position_summary: Russia supports convening a UN-led conference to establish a WMD-free zone in the Middle East, backing UNGA decision 73/546 as a concrete step forward. It blames the United States, United Kingdom, and Canada for obstructing progress and frames the initiative as aligned with the UN’s peace and security mandate.
entries.quote: 7. More than 20 years without any tangible progress in implementing the 1995 resolution has predictably led to growing discontent among the Arab countries of the region. In view of the stalemate, the League of Arab States initiated returning the WMD-free zone issue to “inside the walls” of the United Nations and holding of a relevant conference, facilitated by the Secretary-General of the United Nations. The Russian Federation supported that step, based primarily on the mandate of the United Nations to maintain international peace and security, but also in view of the blatant sabotage of that process by Washington, London and Ottawa, which had aligned itself with them. As a result, the General Assembly adopted decision 73/546 of 23 December 2018 on convening a United Nations conference on the establishment of WMD-free zone in the Middle East, marking the first practical step towards the establishment of a WMD-free zone since 1974.

entries.position_summary: Russia expresses consistent and strong support for establishing a WMD-free zone in the Middle East, calling it a high foreign policy priority. It views the zone as an effective means to strengthen regional security and the broader non-proliferation regime, benefiting all states in and beyond the region.
entries.quote: STATEMENT by the Delegation of the Russian Delegation at the First Session of the Preparatory Committee for the 11th Review Conference of the Parties to the Treaty on the Non-Proliferation of Nuclear Weapons on the Establishment of a Zone Free of Nuclear and Other Weapons of Mass Destruction in the Middle East (Vienna, 7 August 2023) Mr. Chair, The Russian Federation has consistently supported the idea of establishing a zone free of nuclear and other weapons of mass destruction (a WMD-free zone) in the Middle East. This issue has traditionally been high on the list of our foreign policy priorities, since we regard it as an effective way of strengthening regional security and the international non-proliferation regime. The creation of such a zone would be in the interests of all without exception States in the Middle East and beyond.

entries.position_summary: Russia underscores its responsibility as a co-sponsor of the 1995 Resolution on a Middle East WMD-free zone and insists the issue remain on the NPT review agenda until the resolution’s objectives are fully realized.
entries.quote: Russia was one of the co-sponsors of that document. We are fully aware of the responsibility that this status entails for us and believe that the WMD-free zone issue should remain on the agenda of the NPT review process until the goals and objectives of the Resolution are fully achieved.

entries.position_summary: Supports convening and advancing a Middle East WMD-free zone, citing its own initiatives (2012–2013 consultations) and proposal at the 2015 NPT Review Conference. Blames the United States, United Kingdom, and Canada for blocking consensus, which it says stalled progress.
entries.quote: 2 subsequent efforts to convene a conference on a Middle East WMD-free zone with the participation of all States of the region, including the process of informal consultations in Glion and Geneva in 2012-2013 initiated by our country. There was also the 2015 NPT Review Conference, when Russia's proposal served as a basis for the Middle East section of the outcome document. Unfortunately, the emerging consensus was thwarted by the United States, the United Kingdom and Canada — of whom the former two had co-sponsored the Resolution of 1995 — which essentially precluded the NPT forum from making any progress towards the establishment of a WMD-free zone for years to come.

entries.position_summary: The country emphasizes that inclusive regional dialogue, including Israel, is essential to advance a Middle East WMD-free zone. It notes that the 2013–2014 Glion-Geneva consultations nearly agreed on a conference but ultimately did not produce concrete results.
entries.quote: 5. In 2013–2014, for the first time since the Madrid process of the early 1990s, five rounds of informal consultations on the possible convening of a conference on a WMD-free zone were held in Glion and Geneva (Switzerland), in which Israel also participated. At a certain point, the participants almost came to an agreement on the date of a future conference and its organizational arrangements. Unfortunately, however, it was not possible to come up with concrete solutions. Although the Glion- Geneva process did not yield practical results, it demonstrated that dialogue among the countries of the region, including Israel, is possible, and that meaningful progress towards the establishment of a WMD-free zone can only be achieved in this way.

entries.position_summary: The country criticizes the failure to hold the 2012 conference on a Middle East WMD-free zone and accuses the UK, US, and Canada of blocking progress at the 2015 NPT Review Conference. It implies support for establishing the zone and disapproval of those obstructing it.
entries.quote: 6. Nevertheless, the 2012 conference never took place and any further attempts under the Treaty to move towards establishing a WMD-free zone were blocked at the 2015 Review Conference by the United Kingdom and the United States, as well as by Canada, which joined them.
